metadata.career_relevance: API Gateways are the entry point to microservice architectures. Spring Cloud Gateway applies circuit breakers, retries, and rate limiting to ALL incoming requests before they reach any service — centralizing resilience rather than duplicating it in every microservice. This is the production pattern at companies like Razorpay, Flipkart, and Zerodha.

hook.real_world_problem:
Razorpay has 15 microservices. Without a gateway, each service needs its own rate limiter, circuit breaker, and retry logic — duplicated 15 times. With Spring Cloud Gateway:

```yaml
# ONE gateway config protects ALL services:
spring.cloud.gateway.routes:
  - id: payment-service
    uri: lb://payment-service
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/payments/**
    filters:
      - name: CircuitBreaker
        args: { name: payment-cb, fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/payments }
      - name: Retry
        args: { retries: 3, statuses: SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE }
      - name: RequestRateLimiter
        args:
          redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100
          redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 200
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Configure a Spring Cloud Gateway with circuit breaker, retry, and rate limiting filters. Write fallback controllers for graceful degradation at the gateway level.

concept_explanation.gateway_setup:
**Spring Cloud Gateway setup:**

```xml
<!-- pom.xml: -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.cloud</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-cloud-starter-gateway</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.cloud</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-cloud-starter-circuitbreaker-reactor-resilience4j</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-redis-reactive</artifactId>
</dependency>
```

```yaml
# application.yml:
spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      routes:
        - id: payment-service
          uri: lb://payment-service  # load-balanced
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/payments/**
          filters:
            # Rate limiting:
            - name: RequestRateLimiter
              args:
                redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100
                redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 150
                key-resolver: '#{@apiKeyResolver}'
            # Circuit breaker:
            - name: CircuitBreaker
              args:
                name: payment-circuit
                fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/payments
            # Retry:
            - name: Retry
              args:
                retries: 3
                statuses: SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE
                methods: GET
```

visual_aid.description: Gateway as single entry point: Client → Gateway → [Rate Limiter] → [Circuit Breaker] → [Retry] → Microservice. If circuit OPEN: → Fallback Controller → returns degraded response. All 15 microservices have the same gateway protection without needing their own implementations. Redis stores rate limiter counters shared across gateway instances.
visual_aid.alt_text: Spring Cloud Gateway with three filters: rate limiter, circuit breaker, retry protecting all downstream microservices

worked_example.scenario: Configure Spring Cloud Gateway to protect Zerodha's order and market data services with rate limiting, circuit breaker, and retry.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. pom.xml: spring-cloud-starter-gateway + circuit breaker + redis-reactive
2. application.yml: 2 routes with all 3 filters
3. FallbackController for each route
4. KeyResolver bean for API key-based rate limiting

step_by_step_solution.details:
```yaml
spring.cloud.gateway.routes:
  - id: order-service
    uri: lb://order-service
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/orders/**
    filters:
      - name: RequestRateLimiter
        args: { redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 200, redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 300, key-resolver: '#{@userIdResolver}' }
      - name: CircuitBreaker
        args: { name: order-cb, fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/orders }
      - name: Retry
        args: { retries: 2, statuses: SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE }
  - id: market-data
    uri: lb://market-data-service
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/market/**
    filters:
      - name: CircuitBreaker
        args: { name: market-cb, fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/market }
```

worked_example.expected_output:
# Normal request:
GET /api/orders/ORD-001 → gateway → order-service → 200 OK

# Rate limit exceeded:
GET /api/orders/ORD-002 → rate limiter → 429 Too Many Requests

# Order service down:
GET /api/orders/ORD-003 → CB OPEN → fallback → /fallback/orders → 503 Degraded
things_to_notice: Gateway applies filters to EVERY request — no duplication across microservices.
Retry filter: only retries GET requests (safe to retry) and 503 responses. Never retry POST/PUT (non-idempotent).
Redis reactive: gateway uses reactive stack (WebFlux). Use spring-boot-starter-data-redis-reactive, not the standard redis starter.
FallbackController: must be in the SAME gateway application, not in downstream services. It's registered at path /fallback/*.
KeyResolver: determines what uniquely identifies a rate-limit subject. By API key → per-merchant limits. By user ID → per-user limits. By IP → anonymous limits.

industry_examples.use_case: Single gateway entry point for 15 microservices. Rate limiting per merchant API key. Circuit breakers per downstream service. Retry on 503s. Fallback controllers return structured error responses.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you apply resilience patterns in a microservice architecture without duplicating code?' → API Gateway. Spring Cloud Gateway applies circuit breaker, retry, and rate limiter via configuration to all routes. Individual services don't need their own resilience implementations for gateway-level protection.
applications.real_world_workflow: Gateway first: add CB + rate limiter at gateway for all services. Service-specific: add @CircuitBreaker inside services for inter-service calls. Don't duplicate gateway-level resilience inside downstream services.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Configuring Retry filter without specifying methods: GET — POST requests get retried, causing double orders.
common_gaps.remediation: Always specify methods: GET for retry. Never retry POST, PUT, DELETE (non-idempotent). The Retry filter defaults to retrying all methods if methods not specified.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Duplicate orders, payments, or other state-changing operations on retry

key_takeaways: Spring Cloud Gateway: centralized resilience for all microservices
CircuitBreaker filter: fallbackUri for graceful degradation
Retry filter: only for GET (never POST/PUT/DELETE)
RequestRateLimiter filter: Redis-backed, KeyResolver selects rate limit key
FallbackController: in the gateway app, handles /fallback/* paths
KeyResolver: determines rate limit subject (user, API key, IP)

self_check_questions.question: Why should the Retry filter only be applied to GET requests at the gateway level?
self_check_questions.answer: GET requests are idempotent: calling GET /api/orders/123 twice returns the same result without side effects. Retrying a GET on 503 is safe. POST/PUT/DELETE are NOT idempotent: POST /api/orders would create a duplicate order if retried. PUT/DELETE would double-apply updates. At the gateway level, you can't easily detect if a POST was partially processed — so never retry non-GET requests without idempotency key infrastructure.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 19: Microservice Resilience is complete! You've mastered circuit breakers (T19.1), retry + rate limiter (T19.2), bulkhead isolation (T19.3), timeout + fallback (T19.4), and gateway-level resilience (T19.5). Module 20 — Observability covers the patterns for understanding what your service is doing in production.
